source_label: Jameson Lopp — Metal Bitcoin Seed Storage Stress Test
excerpt: Independent fire, corrosion, and crush testing of commercially-available metal seed-storage products. Results vary substantially by construction method (stamped plates vs. punched tiles vs. engraved). The report is updated periodically and is the closest thing to an objective comparison of physical-medium durability for seed phrases.
body:
Source excerpt:
Independent fire, corrosion, and crush testing of commercially-available metal seed-storage products. Results vary substantially by construction method (stamped plates vs. punched tiles vs. engraved). The report is updated periodically and is the closest thing to an objective comparison of physical-medium durability for seed phrases.

---

Cited by leaf "Backup procedures":
The backup question has two parts: what to back up, and where. The "what" is bigger than people often realize. For a single-sig wallet, you need the seed phrase plus the BIP39 passphrase (if you used one) plus the derivation path or descriptor (if your wallet uses non-default conventions). For a multisig, you also need the descriptor itself — the script template that says which keys, in what order — because without it the keys alone cannot reconstruct the wallet [1]. "I have my seed words" is necessary but not always sufficient.

The "where" is about durability and secrecy at the same time. A single paper copy in your house defeats a fire or flood; multiple copies in unrelated geographic locations defeat both. Metal seed-storage products solve the durability half (there are independent stress-test reports comparing them on heat, corrosion, and crushing) but a metal plate stamped with your seed is still readable to anyone who finds it, so storage location matters as much as media [2]. Routine practice: at least two physical copies, in physically separate secure locations, one of them in a form (metal, etched) that survives a house fire.

The failure mode that matters most and gets tested least is recovery rehearsal. Before any meaningful funds go in, sweep the seed into a fresh wallet on a separate device and verify it produces the same first receive address as the original wallet — that's the cheapest, most informative test of "my backup actually works" [3]. Six months later, do it again from the metal copy you've never read since stamping it. The realistic outcome of skipping the rehearsal: years from now, somebody discovers that one of the words was illegible, or the passphrase is misremembered, or a non-standard derivation path was used, and the funds are gone. Backup procedures fail silently. Recovery rehearsal is what makes them fail loudly, while you can still fix them.

---

Cited by leaf "Seed phrases (BIP39)":
A BIP39 seed phrase is a list of 12 or 24 words drawn from a standard 2,048-word list, encoding 128 or 256 bits of entropy plus a checksum [1]. The phrase plus an optional passphrase gets stretched through PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA512 to produce the 512-bit seed that BIP32 derives all keys from. The wordlist is fixed (English is the canonical, others exist but are not always interoperable across wallets), and the checksum is what lets a wallet tell you instantly that you mistyped a word.

The optional passphrase is the most-misunderstood part. It is not a password protecting your seed — it is a 25th word that produces a completely different wallet. Same 24 words + no passphrase = wallet A. Same 24 words + passphrase "foo" = wallet B. Wallet A is what someone sees if they steal your seed alone; wallet B is what you actually have funds on, if that's your setup [2]. There is no way to verify a passphrase is "correct" — every passphrase produces a valid (but possibly empty) wallet. This is also called the "plausible deniability" pattern, and it has the obvious operational risk of being unrecoverable if you forget the passphrase but remember the seed.

The failure modes worth memorizing: photographing or typing your seed into anything connected to the internet defeats the entire point of the architecture; "creative" mnemonic schemes (your own words, encoding your own entropy, splitting words across multiple locations using non-standard sharing) routinely produce coins that nobody can recover; and metal seed-storage products are good, but they're solving the durability problem (fire, flood, time) not the secrecy problem. Standard practice is plain 12 or 24 words written or stamped, stored physically, and tested by recovering an empty wallet from them on a spare device before any funds go in [3].
